title: Intro: Where to start in trading? Part 1
subtitle: Trader role, spot vs futures deep dive, Binance, P2P, leverage, liquidation
outcome: Understand the trader's role, spot vs futures in depth, safe Binance onboarding, and liquidation mechanics.
content: Trading is probability work with defined risk — not a casino or guessing game.
takeaway: Traders earn through discipline and repeatable setups; start spot, add futures only with a plan and stop.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson core idea in your own words
Mark the setup on TradingView without entering a live trade
Log entry, stop, target and risk % in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.title: Who is a trader - in simple words

sections.body: Think of the market as a huge bazaar that never closes. Sellers set prices, buyers bargain, and every second someone agrees to a deal. A trader is a person who deliberately participates in this market in order to make money on price differences: he bought cheaper, sold more expensive - or vice versa, if you know how to sell short. This is not an investor who buys shares of a company for ten years and forgets about them. The trader works with a horizon from several seconds to several weeks and each time makes a decision: to enter, wait or exit. He does not have a “guaranteed salary” - there is a series of transactions where some are profitable, some are losses, and the result depends on how much profitable transactions cover unprofitable ones. A beginner often thinks that a trader is the one who “guessed Bitcoin”. A professional thinks differently: he looks for situations where probability and the risk-to-reward ratio work in his favor, and repeats the same rules hundreds of times. Just as a chef does not prepare random dishes, but follows a recipe, a trader follows a setup - a pre-described scenario with an entry, a stop and a target.

Spot is the simplest start. You deposit USDT, buy BTC, and BTC appears on your balance — like buying apples at a market. You mainly profit from rises unless you already hold coins to sell. There is no futures-style liquidation from leverage: if price halves, your coin is worth half, but the exchange does not auto-close your account. Spot is ideal for learning the UI, limit orders, and how balances move in USDT vs coin. Many pros keep core capital in spot or stables and move only active trading funds to futures.

sections.title: Spot: you are actually buying a coin

sections.body: The spot market is the most understandable mode to start with. You transfer USDT to the exchange, click “Buy BTC”, and Bitcoin appears on your balance. It's like buying apples at the market: if you pay money, the apples are yours. We sold the apples at a higher price and made money. Sold cheaper - lost. On spot, you cannot “bet on the fall” in the classical sense if you don’t have the coin itself: to sell what you don’t have, you need a margin spot or futures. But on spot there is no liquidation in the usual futures sense: if the price fell by fifty percent, you are simply left with a bitcoin that costs half as much - it hurts, but the account is not automatically reset because of the leverage. Spot is ideal for the first weeks: study the interface, learn how to place a limit order, see how the balance in USDT and in the coin changes. Many professionals keep their capital in spot or stablecoins, and transfer only the part that is actively traded to futures.

sections.title: USDT-M futures: a contract, not a coin in a wallet

sections.body: Perpetual futures on Binance are not buying Bitcoin. This is a contract: you agree with the counterparty (the exchange and other traders) to participate in the price movement. Payment is made in USDT. You can go long and make money on growth, or go short and make money on fall. It's like betting that tomorrow's temperature will be higher than today's without buying thermometers. Leverage increases the size of your position relative to your margin. A margin of one hundred thousand rubles with a leverage of 10x gives exposure as if you were trading for a million - but both profit and loss are calculated from this increased position. Leverage does not create money out of thin air; it accelerates both profit and loss. Futures have a funding rate - a periodic payment between longs and shorts that keeps the futures price close to the spot. Once every eight hours, one party pays the other. For a beginner, this is a trifle at a distance of one day, but on a weekly short in a strong uptrend, funding can eat up a noticeable part of the profit.

sections.title: Spot vs futures: an in-depth comparison for a beginner

sections.body: Let's put it into one table in our head. Spot: you own an asset, earnings are mainly based on growth, there is no liquidation from leverage, commissions are usually lower, it’s psychologically simpler - “buy and hold.” Futures: do not own a coin, long and short, leverage, liquidation, funding, higher risk of beginner's error. Why then futures? Because in a falling market, spot does not allow you to make money (without a coin on your balance sheet), but futures does. Because with a smaller deposit you can control a larger position - but this is a double-edged sword. Example: deposit one thousand USDT. On spot you buy BTC for the entire amount - price −5% = −50 USDT. On a futures 10x long on the same exposure, the margin is ~100 USDT, but a move of -5% on the position hits -50 USDT on margin, that is, -50% to the collateral. Another −5% - and if there is no stop, you will be eliminated. At spot, after −10% you still have a coin. Conclusion for a beginner: master spot and risk management - then futures with 2-3x leverage and isolated margin. Not the other way around.

sections.title: Binance: how the exchange works and where to start

sections.body: Binance is one of the largest crypto exchanges in the world. This is a platform for you: registration, KYC verification, replenishment, trading, withdrawal. The interface is divided into Spot, USDT-M Futures, P2P, Earn and more. A beginner needs three zones: Wallet (balances), Spot trading, and later Futures. Go through KYC before topping up: passport, selfie - AML standard. Without KYC, P2P and limits will be stricter. Enable two-factor authentication (Google Authenticator), disable unnecessary API permissions. Do not give your password or codes to anyone. “Binance support” on Telegram who asks for a seed phrase is always a scammer. For the first week, go through the menu without transactions: where is the order history, where is the transfer between spot and futures, where is the liquidation calculator on futures. Trade Master in this course teaches you not to press buttons at random, but to connect theory with a chart in TradingView - this is your difference from applications that give only general advice about saving.

sections.title: P2P: how to deposit rubles or reals on the exchange

sections.body: P2P on Binance - purchasing USDT from another user through exchange escrow. You choose a seller with a high rating and a large number of transactions, transfer rubles via SBP or bank, the seller releases USDT to your wallet. The exchange holds the seller's USDT "as collateral" until you confirm payment. In Brazil, PIX is often used; in Mexico - translations and Mercado Pago; in the Russian Federation - SBP and cards of large banks. Safety rules: do not communicate outside the transaction chat, do not accept “return to another card,” do not mark payment if you did not pay. Fraudsters ask to transfer to a third party - cancellation of the transaction. The first time, buy the minimum amount, make sure that USDT has arrived, then increase it. After P2P, USDT is in your fiat/funding wallet - transfer to Spot to buy coins or to Futures for margin trading. P2P fees are often zero; pay the seller's rate spread.

sections.title: Liquidation: what is it and how not to lose your entire deposit in a minute

sections.body: Liquidation on futures is the forced closure of a position by the exchange when the margin does not cover the loss. Analogy: you borrowed money from a friend for a bet, the market went against you, the friend takes the deposit. The liquidation price depends on the leverage, position size, and margin mode (cross or isolated). Isolated margin - only the collateral of this position is at stake; cross - the entire balance of the futures account. For a beginner, only isolated. 20x leverage on the first trade is the path to liquidation from the usual market noise. Stop loss is an order that closes a position until liquidation. A professional places a stop before entry and knows how much USDT he is risking. If the stop is 0.8% of the price, and the risk per trade is 1% of the deposit, the position size is calculated from these two numbers, and not “by eye”. The calculator on Binance shows the liquidation price - open it before clicking “Buy”. If the liquidation is closer than your stop, the size or leverage is incorrect.

sections.title: How to explain to a friend over a cup of coffee

sections.body: Scenario for a friend with no experience: “Trading is not a guaranteed income. This is a risky job. On the spot you buy Bitcoin as a currency - the rate has fallen, the balance has fallen, but the coin remains. In futures, you bet on a direction with leverage - you can make money on a fall, but the exchange will close the deal if the loss eats up the deposit. I first learn from demos and small amounts, risk no more than one percent of the deposit per transaction, and keep a journal. I don’t get into chats with “100x signals.” If a friend asks “how much will you earn,” the honest answer is: “I don’t know in a day. I know how much I’m willing to lose on one trade.” This distinguishes a trader from a gambler. Ask a friend to open TradingView and show yesterday's high and low BTC - this is the first lesson in reading a chart without money.

Myth one: “I’ll make money faster on futures.” You can lose it faster - the shoulder is symmetrical. Myth two: “The spot is safe, you can hold it without a stop.” Altcoin -80% on spot is not liquidation, but it hurts financially. Myth three: “Short is betrayal or magic.” Shorting a futures contract is an ordinary contract, not a moral choice. Myth four: “Chat signals will replace training.” Someone else's entry without your stop and size is a lottery. Myth number five: “You need a lot of money to start.” For training, an amount is enough that they are willing to write off as experience - often 50–200 USDT on a spot, plus months of practice on a demo. The real advantage of a newbie is not capital, but time to learn and journal discipline. Every myth is removed by practice: one week of observing spot and futures without transactions gives more than a month of signals.

sections.title: Numerical example: same trade on spot and futures

sections.body: Let's fix it with numbers. BTC costs 70,000 USDT. You have 1,000 USDT. Scenario A - spot: buy BTC for 1,000 USDT, receive ~0.0143 BTC. The price increased by 3% to 72,100 - sell, get ~1,030 USDT, profit 30 USDT (+3%). The price fell by 3% to 67,900 - balance ~970 USDT, loss 30 USDT (−3%). You keep the coin. Scenario B - 10x futures: margin 1,000 USDT, position ~10,000 USDT face value. +3% on BTC ≈ +300 USDT to position ≈ +30% to margin (+300 USDT). −3% ≈ −300 USDT (−30% margin). −10% on BTC without a stop – liquidation, margin lost. The same market, the same asset - different instrument, different speed of results. A professional chooses a tool for the task: accumulate a coin - spot; trade the fall or hedge - futures with low leverage and stop. A beginner goes through the following path: spot → paper plans → futures 2–3x → only then more.

Plan for the next three days without haste. Day one: Binance account, KYC, 2FA, walk through the interface. Day two: P2P for a minimum amount or demo TradingView - compare the price of BTC spot and perpetual. Day three: on the 15m chart, mark the last clear high and low, write in a notepad: “If I had bought at the low, I would have placed a stop 0.5% lower” - without an actual trade. Do not open futures with high leverage “to try”. Do not copy deals from Telegram. The next lesson is orders, commissions, Tiger and Vataga terminals, scalping versus swing. You are building a foundation: understanding the tools is more important than the first profitable trade. A profitable deal without rules is an accident that will teach you bad habits.

sections.title: Scenario: leverage and liquidation
sections.body: 500 USDT futures balance. 10x long ETH, 50 USDT margin, ~500 exposure. +2% ETH ≈ +10% on margin. −10% without stop = liquidation. Spot: same 500 buys ETH; −10% leaves coin, account not wiped. Leverage speeds outcomes — stops and isolated margin are mandatory.

Stop before entry, not after pain

title: Intro: Where to start? Part 2
subtitle: Limit/market, maker/taker, funding, terminals, scalping vs swing, 0.5–1% risk
outcome: Understand order types, fees, funding, Tiger/Vataga, and position sizing from risk %.
content: The exchange only sees orders — learn limit, market, and risk math.
takeaway: Scalping needs terminal and low fees; beginners start swing 15m at 0.5–1% risk.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson core idea in your own words
Mark the setup on TradingView without entering a live trade
Log entry, stop, target and risk % in the journal

sections.body:
The exchange only sees orders — not your conviction. A limit order waits at your price and often earns maker fee
by adding liquidity to the book. A market order fills immediately at the best available prices — taker fee plus
possible slippage across multiple levels on size. Analogy: limit is ordering a dish at a listed price and waiting;
market is grabbing whatever is on the counter now, maybe pricier. Beginners hammer market from FOMO and pay spread
and double the fee. Pros use market only when speed matters more than a few ticks of cost. Open order history on
Binance and learn statuses: New, Partially Filled, Filled, Canceled. Check maker/taker rates for your VIP tier before
the first live click. Fifty taker scalps per day can cost 1–2% of deposit weekly in fees alone — edge must exceed that tax.

Partial fills are normal on size — check Partially Filled before panic-canceling with market. OCO links take-profit and stop after basics. Post-only forces maker but may miss — scalper's tradeoff.

Maker posted liquidity; taker removed it. On Binance Futures, maker ~0.02% and taker ~0.04% is a common baseline —
taker is twice as expensive. Funding on perpetuals transfers between longs and shorts every eight hours to anchor
futures near spot. Positive funding means longs pay shorts — overheated upside. Hold a long a week at +0.01% three
times daily and funding can erase a modest target. Intraday traders often flat before funding; swing traders check
CoinGlass or the exchange tab before multi-day holds. Funding is rent on directional exposure, not optional trivia.
Log whether your plan crosses a funding timestamp and estimate USDT impact before entry.

BNB fee discount and VIP tiers matter after you log baseline costs. Compare one week of maker vs taker fees in USDT — the gap funds education or leaks edge.

Binance web UI is enough to learn limits, markets, and stops. Scalpers on 5m need hotkeys, footprint clusters, fast
depth, and tape — Tiger.Trade and Vataga are popular in CIS, connected via trade-only API without withdraw rights.
Beginners should master the exchange and TradingView 15m markup before terminals. Order of learning: exchange →
higher timeframe plan → 5m book → terminal for execution speed. TradingView plans; the terminal clicks — never the reverse.
API keys: trade permission only, IP whitelist, never share withdraw-enabled keys. Terminals save seconds; without book
skills, faster clicks only accelerate losses.

Funding uses notional, not margin. A 5000 USDT long pays on full 5000. Multiply by 21 periods per week on multi-day holds. Never open shorts for funding alone without chart setup.

Scalping is seconds to minutes, high fee load, mandatory book skills. Intraday swing uses 15m–1h, fewer trades, more
planning time. Newbies who start scalping for «fast money» usually donate to fees first. Course recommendation:
first twenty to thirty trades as 15m swing at 0.5% risk with full journal, no terminal. Risk per trade is USDT lost
if stop hits — not position notional. Formula: Size = Risk_USDT / (stop% / 100). Ten losses at 1% risk ≈ −10% account —
survivable. Ten losses at 10% per trade ends the account. First deposit is tuition only — no loans, no revenge deposits.
Pick one style for month one and stick to it so journal stats mean something.

Tiger/Vataga: trade-only API, IP whitelist, practice flatten hotkey on demo. Terminal without journal accelerates mistakes, not profits.

sections.title: Scalping vs swing: two different sports

sections.body:
Scalping - transactions from seconds to several minutes, many commissions, you need a glass, low latency, iron discipline.
Intraday swing - hold for hours, decisions at 15m–1h, fewer trades, more time for plan and journal.
Position trading - days and weeks. A beginner makes a mistake when starting with scalping “because the money is faster” -
faster and draining in the absence of experience in reading the glass and amplitude. Course recommendation: first 20–30 trades -
15m swing with 0.5% risk, full journal, no terminal. Scalping - after lessons on glass, tape and amplitude.
You can't run a marathon without learning to walk. The scalper makes money on small movements minus commissions;
swinger - on larger candles with fewer clicks. Both styles require a stop before entry.
Choose one style for the first month and don't switch out of boredom - otherwise the statistics in the magazine are meaningless.

15m swing: Three to five trades per week is normal. Scalp: thirty a day - a different psyche. Assess your work schedule and sleep: scalping after a night shift is a recipe for overtrading. Choose a style that suits your life, not a YouTube video. Swing statistics are easier for a beginner's journal.

SOL example: deposit 800 USDT, risk 0.5% = 4 USDT. Stop 1.2% → size ≈ 333 USDT. The same 4 USDT risk with a stop of 0.6% → size 667 USDT - twice as many contracts, liquidation is closer. Tightening the stop does not make the trade “safer” without reducing the denomination.

Name the setup in words. Log entry, stop, target. Size from risk percent. Note maker/taker and funding if holding >8h.
Skip on FOMO, greed, or fear. Isolated margin, modest leverage for beginners. No complete checklist — no click.
Trade Master links lesson → TradingView markup → journal; signal groups skip the chart step. After this lesson open
BTCUSDT 5m, measure bid/ask spread, size a hypothetical trade at 1% risk. Next lesson covers amplitude and ATR —
without measurement, stops and targets are random. Professionals skip ten setups a day; amateurs trade boredom.

Futures calculator must show liquidation beyond your stop — else fix leverage or size. Tape the formula on paper week one.

Example on BINANCE:BTCUSDT. Deposit 1000 USDT, risk 1% = 10 USDT. Entry 67000, stop 66330 (−1%).
Stop distance 670 USDT / 67000 = 1%. Size ≈ 10 / 0.01 = 1000 USDT face value. With 5x leverage, the margin is ≈ 200 USDT.
If the stop is 66500 (−0.75%): size ≈ 10 / 0.0075 ≈ 1333 USDT - more contracts for the same risk in money.
If you reduce the risk to 0.5% = 5 USDT with a 1% stop: size 500 USDT. Three levers: risk%, stop distance, leverage.
Leverage does not increase the risk tolerance in USDT - it only compresses the margin. Newbie mistake: “leverage is 10x, so I’m risking 10%” —
no, you set the risk%, leverage - how much margin to block. Table in a notepad: deposit, risk%, stop%, size.

SOL example: deposit 800 USDT, risk 0.5% = 4 USDT. Stop 1.2% → size ≈ 333 USDT. The same 4 USDT risk with a stop of 0.6% → size 667 USDT - twice as many contracts, liquidation is closer. Tightening the stop does not make the trade “safer” without reducing the denomination.

15m swing: Three to five trades per week is normal. Scalp: thirty a day - a different psyche. Assess your work schedule and sleep: scalping after a night shift is a recipe for overtrading. Choose a style that suits your life, not a YouTube video. Swing statistics are easier for a beginner's journal.

sections.title: The first deposit is educational, not the last money

sections.body:
The amount you will lose without panic and without affecting rent, food and debts. For some it’s 50 USDT, for others it’s 500 –
be honest with yourself, not with the influencer. Don't take out a loan for trading. Do not “catch up” the deposit after the minus by doubling the size.
Educational deposit buys experience, magazine and stop habit - not Lamborghini. When the statistics are 50+ transactions with compliance
the rules give an understanding of edge - you can discuss the scale. Until then - micro-size. In the Russian Federation and LATAM replenishment via P2P
in the exchange's escrow chat; never via a link from Telegram. KYC and 2FA - up to the first USDT on the account.
Separate the “wallet for life” and the “wallet for learning” mentally, even if physically one account.

Emotional deposit test: imagine -20% per day. If the thought paralyzes, the deposit is large. Reduce. Learning with a micro amount is more honest than “I’ll log in with 100 USDT and learn.” P2P minimum wage is a normal start in the Russian Federation and Brazil.

Tiger connection: API key only Enable Reading + Enable Futures, IP restrict. Vataga is similar. Do not install the terminal on a VPS in another country for no reason - the delay will kill your scalp. Hotkey “close all” - practice on demo. Terminal without log = quick errors.

sections.title: How to explain to a friend about orders and risk

sections.body:
Scenario for coffee: “Limit order is like ordering in a restaurant: you name the price, you wait. Market - I ate what was given on the menu now,
may be more expensive. On futures, every 8 hours someone pays someone funding - like rent for a bet on a direction.
I risk 1% of the account per trade - ten bad days in a row won't kill me. Scalping is a sprint, swing is a jog in the park;
I'm still learning to run 15m in the park. Tiger and Vataga - for the sprint when I learn to read the glass. I don’t get into chats with 100x.”
If a friend asks “how much will you earn” - honestly: “I don’t know in a day. I know how much I’m willing to lose on one trade.”
Show the bid/ask spread on BTC on your phone - this is a lesson without money.

A friend asks about Tiger - the answer is: “It’s a button accelerator, not magic. Without a stop and a plan, the terminal will only crash faster.” Show the calculation of 1% risk on your phone calculator - it’s clearer than words.

Funding is calculated based on the position's nominal value, not on the margin. Long 5000 USDT with a margin of 500 and funding +0.01% = 0.5 USDT in 8 hours. Few? Multiply by 21 periods per week. A short in a bearish trend with negative funding receives payments - but do not go short “for the sake of funding” without a setup on the chart.

The lesson gives theory. TradingView - level marking and “if the price does X” scenario. Exchange - execution only according to plan.
The magazine connects the three: what was planned on the chart, what was clicked on the stock exchange, what was felt before and after.
Finera and similar apps teach you how to save; they don't teach glass and don't lead to practice on your schedule - a gap,
which closes Trade Master. After this lesson, open BINANCE:BTCUSDT 5m, measure the spread, calculate the size
upon your deposit. The next lesson is amplitude and ATR: without them, stop and take are random numbers.

Link: in the morning TradingView - levels; during the day the stock exchange has a limit according to plan; In the evening, a journal - what we did and what we missed. Finera will not replace this cycle. One miss on the checklist is not a disaster; ten omissions are a player’s habit.

25% discount on commissions when paying with BNB - enable in the settings after you have calculated the base rate without a discount. VIP0→VIP1 requires volume - don’t rush; discipline first. Compare week maker vs taker in the log: the difference in USDT will be surprising. Commissions on spot are lower than on futures - another argument to start with spot.

sections.title: Checklist before your first real deal

sections.body:
1) The setup is named with words (not “looks nice”). 2) Entry, stop, take are recorded in the journal. 3) The size is calculated based on the risk %.
4) Maker/taker commission taken into account. 5) Funding is checked if kept longer than 8 hours. 6) Emotion “greed/fear/FOMO” —
if yes, pass. 7) Isolated margin, leverage no higher than 3x at the start. No point - no click.
A professional can skip ten setups a day; the amateur trades in boredom. Your goal for the week is not profit,
and ten journal entries with a complete plan, at least half without a click (observation). Discipline is measured by absences,
not the number of transactions.

Before the first deal of the week, read the checklist out loud. It sounds stupid - it works. If the “funding” clause is not clear, do not hold the position after 00:00 UTC in the first week. Learn one complication at a time.

Partial execution of the limit is the norm on large volumes: half of the position is at 67,000, half is waiting. The newcomer panics and cancels the balance with the market - again a taker fee. Check the Partially Filled status. OCO (one-cancels-other) on Binance connects take and stop - study after the first ten trades. The post-only limit is guaranteed by the maker, but may not be executed - a conscious choice of the scalper.

title: Price amplitude
subtitle: ATR and BTC vs alts
outcome: Measure amplitude and ATR before entries; size stops for each symbol.
content: Amplitude is your market ruler — without it, stops are random.
takeaway: BTC and alts use different scales; ATR(14) on 15m sizes risk.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson core idea in your own words
Mark the setup on TradingView without entering a live trade
Log entry, stop, target and risk % in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Amplitude - how far the price has moved over the selected period, as a percentage of the price. Open BINANCE:BTCUSDT 15m
and measure the distance from the low to the high of the last hourly candle. If BTC at 80000 has passed 800 points -
This is 1% per hour, which is already noticeable for Bitcoin. On BINANCE:PEPEUSDT for the same hour 8% is a typical Tuesday for the meme-alt.
A beginner puts a take of 0.3% on BTC and on the alt in the same way: on one he is suffocated in noise, on the other he lacks movement.
Amplitude answers: “how long does the market generally go here?” Before looking for an entry, measure the rate for the last 20 candles.
Write in the journal: pair, TF, average range% - this is a ruler for stop and goals for the week ahead.

Ruler tool in TradingView: select the range of the candle - you will see a % in the corner. Practice on ten BTC candles in a row - your eye will get used to 0.2% vs 1%. Without this habit, ATR seems like an abstraction.

Result of the week: re-read the measurement log - which pair hit the narrow stop more often? There, increase the ATR multiplier.

Practice on BINANCE:BTCUSDT 15m: select twenty candles, calculate the average range as a percentage, add ATR(14) and write it in the table. Compare with BINANCE:SOLUSDT and BINANCE:PEPEUSDT on the same day - you will see why the 0.5% stop on PEPE is hit more often. Before each trade, recalculate the ATR after the news; Yesterday's value on FOMC day is irrelevant. The goal of the lesson is the habit of “first the ruler, then the entrance.” Without metering, you pay the spread and lottery commission.

Practice on BINANCE:BTCUSDT 15m: select twenty candles, calculate the average range as a percentage, add ATR(14) and write it in the table. Compare with BINANCE:SOLUSDT and BINANCE:PEPEUSDT on the same day - you will see why the 0.5% stop on PEPE is hit more often. Before each trade, recalculate the ATR after the news; Yesterday's value on FOMC day is irrelevant. The goal of the lesson is the habit of “first the ruler, then the entrance.” Without metering, you pay the spread and lottery commission.

Practice on BINANCE:BTCUSDT 15m: select twenty candles, calculate the average range as a percentage, add ATR(14) and write it in the table. Compare with BINANCE:SOLUSDT and BINANCE:PEPEUSDT on the same day - you will see why the 0.5% stop on PEPE is hit more often. Before each trade, recalculate the ATR after the news; Yesterday's value on FOMC day is irrelevant. The goal of the lesson is the habit of “first the ruler, then the entrance.” Without metering, you pay the spread and lottery commission.

ATR (Average True Range) - the average range of the candle, not the direction. Add ATR(14) at 15m in TradingView.
Value of 120 USDT on BTC at 67000 ≈ 0.18% per 15m candle. Stop already ATR - you will be knocked out by noise; a stop at 2–3×ATR gives space
for normal breathing prices. Take 1×ATR - a modest scalp inside the candle; 2–3×ATR — intraday swing. ATR is growing
on FOMC news, CPI, on listings - recalculate before the session, do not use yesterday's ATR on the day of the report.
On alts, ATR is higher as a percentage - leverage and risk% should be lower than on BTC. ATR doesn't say "where" - only "how wide."

True Range takes into account the gap from the close of the previous candle - ATR is a little more accurate than a simple high-low. On a 24/7 crypto, gaps are rare during the day, but they happen at the opening of the week. It is normal for ATR to reset after a weekend.

Size and ATR: If the ATR widens after news, reduce the denomination so that USDT risk does not increase. Same formula from Lesson 2, new stop%.

BTC as a compass: open 15m BTC in front of any alt. If BTC falls like a knife, long SOL or PEPE is a countertrend with increased risk. Magazine: column “BTC trend 15m” next to each alt idea. In a month you will see how many stops were due to ignoring the context. These are free statistics if you write them down honestly.

In a month you will see how many stops were due to ignoring the context. These are free statistics if you write them down honestly.

sections.title: BTC vs alts: different speeds of the same market

sections.body:
BTC is a locomotive: capital first there, less than % amplitude at 15m on a calm day (0.3–0.8% typical).
ETH, SOL - middle class: 1-3% for 15m in an active session. PEPE, ARB - high volatility: 5–15% per session is not uncommon.
Alts often move with a multiplier to BTC: BTC +1%, SOL +2–3% in alt season. Before going long alts, look at BTCUSDT 15m -
if Bitcoin falls, alt will rarely save your long. Do not copy the 0.5% stop from BTC to PEPE: there is more noise there, the stop will work
on a random tick. Rate rule: a separate line in the BTC context log before each alt trade.

Correlation: when BTC falls 2% in an hour, SOL could be -4%, PEPE -8%. The multiplier is not constant - in the alt season, alts grow faster. Table in the journal: “BTC −1%, SOL ?%, PEPE ?%” on the day of the event.

To a friend: “First the ruler, then the doors (levels). Without a ruler, you don’t know whether the door is high or low.”

Leverage on a volatile coin is an accelerator of liquidation, no profit. Example: ATR PEPE 3%, stop 0.5% - you will be knocked out by noise; Stop 2% with a risk of 1% USDT gives a lower denomination - and rightly so. The Binance calculator will show liquidation - check to Buy. Memes: 2-3x or training spot.

Leverage on a volatile coin is an accelerator of liquidation, no profit. Example: ATR PEPE 3%, stop 0.5% - you will be knocked out by noise; Stop 2% with a risk of 1% USDT gives a lower denomination - and rightly so. The Binance calculator will show liquidation - check to Buy. Memes: 2-3x or training spot.

sections.title: Leverage and amplitude - why the 20x meme kills faster

sections.body:
High amplitude + high leverage = elimination of normal noise. BTC 5x at 15m with a stop for 1.5×ATR - tolerable for training.
PEPE 20x with the same % stop - liquidation is closer than your plan. The formula is the same: the risk in USDT is fixed, but the volatility
dictates the width of the foot, and the width of the foot dictates the size. On a volatile asset with the same USDT risk, the position is less in nominal value,
but the beginner increases his leverage “to earn more” - and receives a margin call. Memes and low-liquidity pairs: 2–3x or spot.
The liquidation calculator on Binance is a mandatory step before Buying on alt.

Liquidation cascade on the meme: ATR doubles in minutes - your yesterday’s stop no longer corresponds to the market. ATR recalculation before each session, more than once a month. In the news, sit to the side or micro-size.

Error “I don’t look at ATR, I look only at levels” - the level on PEPE can be wider than 5%, a stop of 0.5% is meaningless. Levels and ATR together.

Take profit without reference to the session range creates the illusion of being “almost in the black”. Measure a typical stroke over 4 hours; 0.3% take on BTC on a calm day is achievable; on SOL on the same day - not enough. Partial fixation at +1R removes greed. Write down in your plan: “take = X% = Y×ATR.”

Take profit without reference to the session range creates the illusion of being “almost in the black”. Measure a typical stroke over 4 hours; 0.3% take on BTC on a calm day is achievable; on SOL on the same day - not enough. Partial fixation at +1R removes greed. Write down in your plan: “take = X% = Y×ATR.”

sections.title: Take profit for the session amplitude

sections.body:
Unrealistic take is the reason “almost in the black all the time, but in the minus.” Measure the average range of the last 20 candles to 15m.
Take 0.5× session amplitude - conservative scalp; 1–1.5× — intraday swing after the setup. Greed "5% per hour without
news" is not a plan, but a lottery. If the nearest resistance is less than the planned take, cut the target or cancel the setup.
Partial fixation at +1R relieves all-or-nothing pressure. Write down in the plan: “take is based on ATR/range = X%” - then
compare with the fact in the magazine.

Take 2xATR on BTC 15m on a calm day - realistic. Take 0.3×ATR on PEPE is possible, but the commissions will eat up. Count net after taker×2 + spread.

Practice: Excel spreadsheet/notepad - three pairs, ATR%, range 20, your stop in ×ATR. Goal: Stop 1.5–3×ATR everywhere, not 0.5×ATR on a volatile coin.

The error of copying stops from BTC to alts is the top reason for early stops for beginners. Table in the journal: pair, ATR%, stop%, stop/ATR ratio. Goal: stop 1.5–3×ATR on each pair. If the ratio is <1, the stop is too narrow.

The error of copying stops from BTC to alts is the top reason for early stops for beginners. Table in the journal: pair, ATR%, stop%, stop/ATR ratio. Goal: stop 1.5–3×ATR on each pair. If the ratio is <1, the stop is too narrow.

Open BINANCE:BTCUSDT and BINANCE:SOLUSDT at 15m side by side. For each: high-low of the last 20 candles in %, ATR(14) in %.
Compare with your typical stop. If the stop is narrower than the middle range, you are trading noise. Repeat for PEPEUSDT - you will see the difference
scales Take a screenshot with the signatures. No deal - just measurement. This is 20 minutes that will save your deposit from random numbers.
Trade Master teaches you to measure before entering; signal chats give “enter now” without a line - hence the different results after a month.

Take 2xATR on BTC 15m on a calm day - realistic. Take 0.3×ATR on PEPE is possible, but the commissions will eat up. Count net after taker×2 + spread.

To a friend without jargon: “Bitcoin has a shorter step, a meme has a wider step - first we measure the step of the candle, then we set doors (levels) and stops.” Show three graphs side by side - visuals are more convincing than numbers.

The same take for all coins. Ignore BTC when trading alt. Leverage like BTC on the meme. Stop “by eye” without %.
Averaging on a falling alt without recalculating ATR. Trading in the first minutes of listing with a size similar to BTC.
Correction: table in the journal - pair, ATR%, average range 20 candles, stop%, take%, ratio to ATR.
After 30 recordings you will see where the feet are too narrow.

Error “I don’t look at ATR, I look only at levels” - the level on PEPE can be wider than 5%, a stop of 0.5% is meaningless. Levels and ATR together.

Liquidation cascade on the meme: ATR doubles in minutes - your yesterday’s stop no longer corresponds to the market. ATR recalculation before each session, more than once a month. In the news, sit to the side or micro-size.

Link to Lesson 2: Position size from USDT risk does not change; The stop distance from ATR changes. Wide ATR → wide stop → fewer contracts. Don’t narrow your stop on PEPE “to fit more” - the math is against you.

sections.title: How to explain amplitude to a friend

sections.body:
“Bitcoin has one walking speed, a cheap coin has a sprint. First we measure how much the coin moves in an hour, then we set
stop and target. ATR is the average step of the candle. If the stop is less than the step, we will be thrown out by a random market sneeze. I'm not guessing -
I adjust the plan to the traffic norm.” Show three charts: BTC, SOL, PEPE - one TF, different width of candles. A friend will understand without formulas.

To a friend: “First the ruler, then the doors (levels). Without a ruler, you don’t know whether the door is high or low.”

Correlation: when BTC falls 2% in an hour, SOL could be -4%, PEPE -8%. The multiplier is not constant - in the alt season, alts grow faster. Table in the journal: “BTC −1%, SOL ?%, PEPE ?%” on the day of the event.

Homework: three pairs, 15m, range 20 candles + ATR to the journal without clicking. The next lesson is support for loys on the same candles. The lesson is completed when you say the ATR% of BTC out loud.

sections.title: ATR, position size and risk in USDT

sections.body:
Wide ATR → wider stops → fewer contracts for the same USDT risk. Example: risk 10 USDT, stop 0.5% on BTC - size 2000 USDT.
On PEPE, the stop is 2% for the same risk - size 500 USDT. Do not narrow your stop on a volatile coin “to fit in more” -
will knock you out. Either reduce your risk % or choose a less volatile pair to learn from. ETHUSDT is a good bridge between BTC and alts.

Size and ATR: If the ATR widens after news, reduce the denomination so that USDT risk does not increase. Same formula from Lesson 2, new stop%.

True Range takes into account the gap from the close of the previous candle - ATR is a little more accurate than a simple high-low. On a 24/7 crypto, gaps are rare during the day, but they happen at the opening of the week. It is normal for ATR to reset after a weekend.

Trade Master: theory without TradingView is forgotten. Screenshot with ATR and range - in the log folder. Finera teaches you how to save; we teach how to measure movement before a click.

sections.title: Result: amplitude before entry, log after

sections.body:
Amplitude and ATR are not “for advanced”, but the basic line for beginners. BTC and alts are different scales; BTC context is required.
The next lesson is support for loys: levels are built on the same candles that you already know how to measure. Homework: three pairs,
15m, range 20 candles + ATR in the magazine. No click on the exchange. When you can say out loud the ATR% for BTC, you are ready for levels.

Result of the week: re-read the measurement log - which pair hit the narrow stop more often? There, increase the ATR multiplier.

Ruler tool in TradingView: select the range of the candle - you will see a % in the corner. Practice on ten BTC candles in a row - your eye will get used to 0.2% vs 1%. Without this habit, ATR seems like an abstraction.

Recap of the week: re-read the measurement log. Which pair hit the narrow stop more often? Increase the ATR multiplier there. Market 24/7 - take your time with your leverage.

title: Support by lows
subtitle: Swing lows, false breaks, level strength
outcome: Build support from swing lows and trade false breaks with rules.
content: Support is market memory — needs touches and reaction candles.
takeaway: Zones not pennies; false break confirmed by 15m close.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson core idea in your own words
Mark the setup on TradingView without entering a live trade
Log entry, stop, target and risk % in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Swing low is a local minimum, after which the price bounced up and formed a new mini-maximum.
On BINANCE:ETHUSDT 15m, find three such points over the past week. Not every long lower shadow is a loy: it needs a rebound,
confirmed by the following candles. Analogy: the floor in an apartment, from which the ball bounced upward several times - not every scratch is on the tile.
One way is observation. Two touches is the idea of ​​the level. Three or more is the zone where the market “remembers” the price. Beginner draws twenty lines
under every shadow; the professional leaves three to five levels per session. Fewer lines mean more clarity in plan.

Loy on a higher TF is stronger: loy on ETH 1h is more important than loy on 5m. Practice: mark one 1h loy and check how many times 15m respected him.

The next lesson, glass, will explain who is at the bid for your support - a combination of level + glass.

Building support on BINANCE:ETHUSDT: find three loys at 15m in a week, draw a zone, note the number of touches. The senior 1h loy is more important than the 5m shadow. A single style—shadows or bodies—across the entire chart. Log: date, zone, touches, outcome. A month later - statistics on rebounds vs breakouts.

A single style—shadows or bodies—across the entire chart. Log: date, zone, touches, outcome. A month later - statistics on rebounds vs breakouts.

A single style—shadows or bodies—across the entire chart. Log: date, zone, touches, outcome. A month later - statistics on rebounds vs breakouts.

A single style—shadows or bodies—across the entire chart. Log: date, zone, touches, outcome. A month later - statistics on rebounds vs breakouts.

sections.title: How to build support by loyalty

sections.body:
Algorithm for 15m/1h: find three significant levels at the same height ±0.1–0.3% (zone, not a penny). Draw a horizontal line.
Decide on a single style: based on candle bodies or shadow extremes - and stick to it throughout the entire chart. ETH often respects zones
wider than BTC. The higher timeframe (1h) is more important than the younger one (5m): the level with 1h is stronger than a random loy at 5m.
Support is not a “magic line”, but the market’s memory of the price where buyers previously entered. When the price comes up again,
The question is: will buyers appear again or is the level depleted? Log: date of construction, number of touches, pair.

Zone 2450–2455 vs line 2452.3 - on the exchange execution at the market price, not at your line. The zone reduces false “misses”.

Log of levels in a month: what% of rebounds vs breakouts? If it’s 50/50, you’re building the levels correctly, you need an input filter.

False breakout: shadow below the level, close above - hunting stops. Entry after closing 15m, not on the first tick. Stop below the false breakout extreme with a margin of 0.2–0.5% on ETH. A log of each false breakout is a training material.

False breakout: shadow below the level, close above - hunting stops. Entry after closing 15m, not on the first tick. Stop below the false breakout extreme with a margin of 0.2–0.5% on ETH. A log of each false breakout is a training material.

sections.title: Strength level depending on the number of touches

sections.body:
More touches mean more attention from traders to the level, but also a greater chance of a breakdown: liquidity below the level is collected by stops.
The first touch after a long rise is often a rebound. The fifth touch in a day is a risk of “wiping” the floor. Record in your journal:
“touch number 3 from the level of 2450 ETH.” Over time, you will see at which touch your plans work more often. Don't enter just because
that “there is a level” - a scenario is needed: a rebound with a reaction of the candle or a false breakout with a closing back.

Touch #4–5: often before a breakout. Don’t add “because you’ve already held it four times” to a long without BTC volume and context.

Show a friend a false breakout on ETH - a visual teaches better definition.

Entry at support requires a candle reaction and a calm BTC. Without reaction - catching the knife. The level limit is maker fee and control. R:R minimum 1:2. Risk 0.5–1% from lesson 2.

sections.title: False breakout downwards - hunting for stops

sections.body:
False breakout: the price goes below the shadow support, but closes back above the level. Classic hunt for stop longs,
standing exactly under the layer. A beginner places a stop 1 tick below the minimum - it is taken before the reversal. Professional puts
stop below the false breakout extreme or below the zone with a margin of 0.2–0.5% on ETH 15m. Entering a long position - after the candle closes back
above the level, not on the first tick up. There are 5m more false breakouts - filter by closing 15m. Screenshot of each false breakout into the log.

False breakout + volume: sometimes the volume on the downside shadow is high - the stops were collected, then the buyback was made. Wait for the closure, don't catch the bottom of the shadow.

Limit for buying from support: sometimes it will not be fulfilled - the price has turned higher. This is not a mistake; mistake - the market is in panic.

Timeframes: 1h zone, 15m trigger, 5m timing. Don't draw 20 lines over 5m. Maximum 3–5 levels per session.

sections.title: Shadows vs bodies - a single markup style

sections.body:
If you build according to shadows, count your feet and touches according to the shadows. If it's by body, don't mix it up. Mixing gives "a level that
contradicts himself." On volatile alts, the shadows are longer - the zone is wider. BTC at 15m is often more accurate. Compare BINANCE:ETHUSDT
and BINANCE:BTCUSDT in the same area: ETH’s support zones are visually thicker. This is not a bug - it's the nature of the tool.
Before you trade, ask, “Does my stop match the line style?”

Candle bodies during a flat, shadows during volatility - flexibility without changing the style every candle.

5m for entry after signal 15m - approx. 5m for “find support from scratch” is noise.

Broken support often becomes resistance during a retest - write it down for future lessons. Update levels weekly.

levels from scratch. Mistake: draw support at 5m and ignore that at 1h the price is empty. Rule: senior TF sets
zone, junior - timing. If at 1h the level is far away, at 5m the scalp to this support may be early.

sections.title: Login from support - scenario, not hope

sections.body:
Minimum: level + reaction (pin bar, engulfing, range narrowing) + BTC does not fall like a knife. Stop below the zone, not inside it.
Take at least 1:2 to the risk or at the nearest resistance. Without a reaction, a candle is “catching a falling knife”, not level trading.
Risk 0.5–1% of the deposit, size from the formula in lesson 2. Limit entry at the level is preferable to the market - maker fee and price control.

Limit for buying from support: sometimes it will not be fulfilled - the price has turned higher. This is not a mistake; mistake - the market is in panic.

sections.body:
“Imagine a floor from which the ball has bounced three times. False shot - the ball rolled slightly under the plinth and returned.
I don’t buy just because there is a floor - I wait for the ball to bounce off it, and put a stop if it’s been a serious roll.”

sections.title: The glass is like a living bazaar

sections.body:
Order book - list of limit orders: bid (buyers) on the left, ask (sellers) on the right. The price goes where they meet
market orders with limits. On BINANCE:BTCUSDT 5m, open the order book next to the chart. Analogy: bazaar - sellers shout the price,
buyers bargain; transaction - when there is a handshake. The graph shows the total; glass - who is standing in line right now.
A beginner looks only at the candles and wonders “where the reversal comes from” - often from a large bid wall or the removal of an ask wall.

Binance Depth of Depth: 20 levels on the web, more in the terminal. See not only the best bid, but the bid amount of 0.1% of the price.

Practice glass: open the lesson symbol on TradingView, find an example for 7 days. After the stop there is a pause of 15–30 minutes without rematch.

Result: chart + order book + tape - scalper’s triple.

P2P to escrow; KYC and 2FA before deposit. Screenshot of block 10 with signatures - in a journal with the date.

The best bid is the highest purchase price. The best ask is the lowest selling price. Spread = ask − bid. On BTC during liquid hours the spread is pennies;
on a thin viola - interest, and market entry is expensive. Write down the spread in USDT and in % before the scalp. If the spread eats up a third of the planned
take - the setup is dead. The scalper earns a move greater than the spread plus two commissions. Measure every session.

The aggregated order book on BTC is liquid; there are holes on the ARB at night. Don't scalp illiquid assets.

Write down your entry, stop, take and risk of 0.5-1% USDT before clicking. In a fast market, cut the size by 50% for the same USDT risk.

A screenshot of the stack in a journal is required for scalp ideas.

A wall is a large volume at one bid or ask level. Can hold the price (real buyers) or disappear when approached (spoofing).
Do not trade “from the wall” without tape confirmation: the price may break if the wall is removed. On BTC, walls of 50–200 BTC are noticeable;
on ETH - proportionally. Screenshot of a stack with the caption “wall bid 67200” in a magazine is a habit before scalping.

Wall 100 BTC at 67200 – ~6.7M USDT. Compare with the average volume of a minute in the feed.

Check BINANCE:BTCUSDT 15m before trading on alt. The limit at the level is better than the market - maker fee.

Spoofing: a large limit is set, the market reacts, the limit is removed before execution. Prohibited on traditional markets, on crypto
meets. Signs: the wall appears suddenly, far from the price, disappears upon approach; there are no deals in the feed at this level.
Newbie rule: don’t enter just because of the wall - wait for the price reaction and volume in the feed. A wall + a breakdown + a retest is stronger than a wall alone.

Spoofing is caught by the absence of transactions: there is a wall, the tape is empty - suspicion.

The feed is the flow of executed transactions: green purchases by ask, red sales by bid. Aggressive purchases below the level are a bullish sign;
a series of reds without a rebound - pressure. At 5m BTC the tape flashes quickly - see clusters in 10-30 seconds at the level.
Tiger/Vataga show a feed larger than the web interface. Without the tape, the glass is a static picture; with tape - cinema.

Ribbon: large green prints on the level - real demand.

sections.body:
Level on TradingView 15m + glass at 5m at the same level. Plan: “if the bid holds at 67200 and the tape is green, go long with a stop below.”
Contradiction: on the chart there is support, in the order book there is pressure - caution. Trade Master teaches both pictures; chats only give “log in now”.

sections.title: How to explain to a friend about the glass

sections.body:
“The chart is transactions that have already happened. Glass - who is in line to buy and sell. Spread is the difference between the best buy and best sell.
A big wall is like a line of ten people; It may be real, or it may disperse when you approach.”

To a friend: there is a line at the cash register - whoever is first is fulfilled.

title: Fast vs slow market
subtitle: Plan before click, 0.5–1% risk
outcome: Master 'Fast vs slow market' with TradingView practice.
content: Beginner lesson: fast vs slow market.
takeaway: Process beats one lucky trade on Fast vs slow market.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson core idea in your own words
Mark the setup on TradingView without entering a live trade
Log entry, stop, target and risk % in the journal

references.title: Technical Analysis of the Financial Markets
references.author: John J. Murphy

sections.body:
Fast market: wide candles, the spread is widening, the order book is shaking, the tape has no time to read. News, listing, cascade liquidations. Rule: size −50%, stop is wider in % but USDT risk is the same, fewer transactions. On BINANCE:BTCUSDT after CPI 15m candles at 3x normal ATR is a fast mode, not your usual 0.3% take. Wait 15-30 minutes after volatility for the ATR to contract.

There is no VIX crypto, but ATR(14)/average ATR for the month >1.5 - fast mode.

Practice "market mode": open the lesson symbol on TradingView, find an example for 7 days. After the stop there is a pause of 15–30 minutes without rematch.

Practice: two BTC screens - ATR day high and low.

Slow market: narrow range, thin order book, low volume - Asian session, Sunday. False breakouts are more common; takes are modest. The mistake is overtrading out of boredom. A pass is better than ten trades in a narrow flat with commissions. Measure the range of 20 candles - if it is less than your stop, do not trade the breakout.

Sunday UTC evening - thin market, false breakouts on BTC.

sections.title: False impulses in a fast market

sections.body:
A long candle without continuation is a hunt for FOMO. Do not chase the market into the tail of the candle. Wait for a retest or a second candle. On SOLUSDT in the news this is especially tough.

Slow flat: take on the opposite border of the range, not the middle.

Screenshot of block 4 with signatures - in a journal with the date. Explain the setup to a friend in a minute without promising profit.

Stop in a fast market is behind the extreme of the impulse candle, not inside the body.

sections.body:
Stop - admitting an error before entering. Without a stop, one trade can wipe out a month. On futures without a stop, you will be closed by liquidation - worse than a planned stop. “I’ll bet later” = no deal. Record the stop in your journal before the click.

Stop loss on the stock exchange, not in your head - Conditional order or OCO.

Lower highs converge towards support - pressure increases.

sections.body:

title: Channels: parallel support and resistance

sections.body: “A swing between two pillars: buy near the floor, sell near the ceiling, before the ball hits the wall.” Let a friend draw the lines on TradingView - motor memory is more important than reading.

Channel on senior TF? Three taps? Price at the border? Stop behind the channel, risk ≤1%? The target is on the opposite wall? One “no” - watch. One quality deal is better than five in the middle.

title: Uptrend: Higher Highs and Higher Lows

content: An uptrend is a series of higher highs and higher lows.
takeaway: In uptrends, buy pullbacks to HL — not chase every HH.

sections.body: “Staircase down: each bounce is lower than the previous one, each low is lower. Don’t jump from the middle of the fall - wait for the rebound to the ceiling (LH).”

title: Altseason explained
subtitle: BTC dominance and capital rotation
outcome: Read BTC.D and trade alts with rules—not FOMO.
content: Altseason = alts outperform BTC as dominance falls.
takeaway: BTC and BTC.D first—then your alt.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson core idea in your own words
Mark the setup on TradingView without entering a live trade
Log entry, stop, target and risk % in the journal

sections.body:
Altseason and BTC dominance. Trading is a continuous 24/7 auction where buyers and sellers meet on price every second.
Define entry, stop, target, and risk 0.5–1% of deposit before every click — not after you are already in the trade.
One winning trade does not prove genius; one losing trade does not prove the market is unfair. Edge appears only
over dozens of repetitions logged with screenshots and honest emotion tags. Beginners chase green candles;
professionals wait for their predefined setup and pass everything else. BTC context matters for nearly every
crypto decision because capital rotates through Bitcoin before most alt moves. Treat each session as practice
reps toward a hundred-trade sample, not as a lottery ticket that must pay today. Write the plan in plain language
a non-trader friend could understand — jargon often hides missing logic.

Measure recent range in percent on your working timeframe before sizing. Use ATR(14) or the high-low of the last
twenty candles as a ruler for stop distance and target realism. If distance to invalidation is smaller than normal
noise, skip — you are paying spread to gamble. High amplitude pairs demand lower leverage and smaller risk percent.
Segment journal statistics by setup name and session, not by coin ticker alone. A broken strategy on PEPE will
break on the next meme; a solid retest rule travels across liquid pairs. Compare spread to stop distance on
illiquid alts — if spread eats more than five percent of planned risk, choose a more liquid symbol or reduce size.

Open the lesson symbol at the stated interval. Mark swing highs and lows, sessions, or pattern boundaries with
horizontal lines and text labels for entry, stop, and target. Take one screenshot before any live order and store
it in your journal folder dated by session. Write setup name, planned R:R, and emotion score from one to ten before
entry. No screenshot means no trade — memory lies after three consecutive losses. Trade Master links lesson theory
to chart markup to journal review; signal groups skip the middle step and sell hope instead of process.

Limit orders add liquidity and often pay maker fee; market orders remove liquidity as taker — fees compound fast
for active scalpers. Funding every eight hours on perpetual contracts affects holds longer than one session.
Beginners use isolated margin only with modest leverage. On macro news days such as FOMC or CPI, stay flat thirty
minutes before release unless you have years of experience. Never average down without a written max loss in USDT
and an intact technical thesis. Daily loss limit and three-strike stop rule protect you from revenge spirals.

Trading without a stop, all-in on one illiquid coin, FOMO chase after missing a trigger, copying chat signals,
revenge size after a stop-out, moving stops wider to avoid being wrong, overtrading during fatigue hours after work.
Each mistake has a structural fix: checklist before click, cooldown timer after stop, tuition deposit size only,
weekly review by setup expectancy. If you cannot explain the trade idea to a friend in sixty seconds without
mentioning guaranteed profit, you are not ready to click live. Scam chats promise returns to harvest deposits;
legitimate education shows chart process and risk math instead. Protect API keys, enable 2FA, and never send USDT
to strangers for managed trading.

Close the lesson by opening TradingView on the lesson symbol, marking levels without clicking buy or sell,
and writing three sentences in your journal about what you would do if price reaches your trigger. Review
those notes in seven days — improvement in wording and patience often appears before balance growth.
Trading education is decision-making under uncertainty, not hunting lucky tickers. Every professional was
once a beginner with the same questions; the gap is journal discipline, defined stops, and refusing
third-party guarantees of profit. Schedule your next chart review on the calendar like a class — consistency
beats weekend binge studying. Share your written plan with an accountability partner if possible; explaining
aloud exposes gaps in logic before money is at risk.

Lesson 25 on Altseason and BTC dominance is complete when you can explain the idea to a friend in two minutes
and show a marked TradingView screenshot with no open position. Log main takeaway, one risk you will remove
this week, and the date of your next plan review. Do not skip to the next lesson until practice steps are done.
In LATAM, fund via exchange P2P with escrow only; in Russia the same KYC path applies. Competitors may teach
savings habits; Trade Master teaches repeatable chart setups with honest statistics over time. Repeat the
weekly journal review even on weeks with zero trades — observation builds context. Keep position size small
until fifty logged trades follow your written rules. Paper trade or minimum risk when testing a new rule for the first week.
Is BTC stable? BTC.D not growing? Liquidity? Plan and risk? No FOMO? Two “no” is a pass.
Altseason is part of the cycle of accumulation→growth→rotation→euphoria→decline. Take profit.
“When Bitcoin rests sideways, money goes to alts - dominance falls. I look at this before buying any viola."

Lesson 25 is completed when you can explain in your own words the topic “What is an altseason and when to expect it” to a friend in two minutes and show a screenshot of the markup in TradingView without an open position. Write down in your journal: the main conclusion, the one risk you will eliminate this week, and the date of the next review of the plan. Do not move on to the next lesson until you have completed the practice steps - otherwise the theory will be forgotten within a day.

Define entry, stop, target, and risk 0.5–1% of deposit before every click — not after you are already in the trade.
One winning trade does not prove genius; one losing trade does not prove the market is unfair. Edge appears only
over dozens of repetitions logged with screenshots and honest emotion tags. Beginners chase green candles;
professionals wait for their predefined setup and pass everything else. BTC context matters for nearly every
crypto decision because capital rotates through Bitcoin before most alt moves. Treat each session as practice
reps toward a hundred-trade sample, not as a lottery ticket that must pay today. Write the plan in plain language
a non-trader friend could understand — jargon often hides missing logic.

Measure recent range in percent on your working timeframe before sizing. Use ATR(14) or the high-low of the last
twenty candles as a ruler for stop distance and target realism. If distance to invalidation is smaller than normal
noise, skip — you are paying spread to gamble. High amplitude pairs demand lower leverage and smaller risk percent.
Segment journal statistics by setup name and session, not by coin ticker alone. A broken strategy on PEPE will
break on the next meme; a solid retest rule travels across liquid pairs. Compare spread to stop distance on
illiquid alts — if spread eats more than five percent of planned risk, choose a more liquid symbol or reduce size.

Open the lesson symbol at the stated interval. Mark swing highs and lows, sessions, or pattern boundaries with
horizontal lines and text labels for entry, stop, and target. Take one screenshot before any live order and store
it in your journal folder dated by session. Write setup name, planned R:R, and emotion score from one to ten before
entry. No screenshot means no trade — memory lies after three consecutive losses. Trade Master links lesson theory
to chart markup to journal review; signal groups skip the middle step and sell hope instead of process.

Limit orders add liquidity and often pay maker fee; market orders remove liquidity as taker — fees compound fast
for active scalpers. Funding every eight hours on perpetual contracts affects holds longer than one session.
Beginners use isolated margin only with modest leverage. On macro news days such as FOMC or CPI, stay flat thirty
minutes before release unless you have years of experience. Never average down without a written max loss in USDT
and an intact technical thesis. Daily loss limit and three-strike stop rule protect you from revenge spirals.

Trading without a stop, all-in on one illiquid coin, FOMO chase after missing a trigger, copying chat signals,
revenge size after a stop-out, moving stops wider to avoid being wrong, overtrading during fatigue hours after work.
Each mistake has a structural fix: checklist before click, cooldown timer after stop, tuition deposit size only,
weekly review by setup expectancy. If you cannot explain the trade idea to a friend in sixty seconds without
mentioning guaranteed profit, you are not ready to click live. Scam chats promise returns to harvest deposits;
legitimate education shows chart process and risk math instead. Protect API keys, enable 2FA, and never send USDT
to strangers for managed trading.

Close the lesson by opening TradingView on the lesson symbol, marking levels without clicking buy or sell,
and writing three sentences in your journal about what you would do if price reaches your trigger. Review
those notes in seven days — improvement in wording and patience often appears before balance growth.
Trading education is decision-making under uncertainty, not hunting lucky tickers. Every professional was
once a beginner with the same questions; the gap is journal discipline, defined stops, and refusing
third-party guarantees of profit. Schedule your next chart review on the calendar like a class — consistency
beats weekend binge studying. Share your written plan with an accountability partner if possible; explaining
aloud exposes gaps in logic before money is at risk.

Lesson 29 on Marathon block 1 — ETH 15m markup and plan is complete when you can explain the idea to a friend in two minutes
and show a marked TradingView screenshot with no open position. Log main takeaway, one risk you will remove
this week, and the date of your next plan review. Do not skip to the next lesson until practice steps are done.
In LATAM, fund via exchange P2P with escrow only; in Russia the same KYC path applies. Competitors may teach
savings habits; Trade Master teaches repeatable chart setups with honest statistics over time. Repeat the
weekly journal review even on weeks with zero trades — observation builds context. Keep position size small
until fifty logged trades follow your written rules. Paper trade or minimum risk when testing a new rule for the first week.
One screen with entry/stop/target signatures. File name: date_ETH_setup.
Place an alert at the entry level. Before the alert, don’t look at every candle.
You didn't click Buy. You've done a professional's job. Block 2 - Execution and Journal.

Lesson 29 is complete when you can explain the topic “Marathon” in your own words. Block 1" to a friend in two minutes and show a screenshot of the markup in TradingView without an open position. Write down in your journal: the main conclusion, the one risk you will eliminate this week, and the date of the next review of the plan. Do not move on to the next lesson until you have completed the practice steps - otherwise the theory will be forgotten within a day.

title: Averaging down dangers
subtitle: When allowed, when taboo
outcome: Why 'buying cheaper' often speeds liquidation instead of saving a trade.
content: Averaging without loss cap averages account to zero.
takeaway: No plan and cap — do not average.
learningOutcomes: Explain the lesson core idea in your own words
Mark the setup on TradingView without entering a live trade

takeaway: Flat before; planned reaction after.

Log entry, stop, target and risk % in the journal

sections.body:
FOMC Fed meeting trading rules. Trading is a continuous 24/7 auction where buyers and sellers meet on price every second.
Define entry, stop, target, and risk 0.5–1% of deposit before every click — not after you are already in the trade.
One winning trade does not prove genius; one losing trade does not prove the market is unfair. Edge appears only
over dozens of repetitions logged with screenshots and honest emotion tags. Beginners chase green candles;
professionals wait for their predefined setup and pass everything else. BTC context matters for nearly every
crypto decision because capital rotates through Bitcoin before most alt moves. Treat each session as practice
reps toward a hundred-trade sample, not as a lottery ticket that must pay today. Write the plan in plain language
a non-trader friend could understand — jargon often hides missing logic.

Measure recent range in percent on your working timeframe before sizing. Use ATR(14) or the high-low of the last
twenty candles as a ruler for stop distance and target realism. If distance to invalidation is smaller than normal
noise, skip — you are paying spread to gamble. High amplitude pairs demand lower leverage and smaller risk percent.
Segment journal statistics by setup name and session, not by coin ticker alone. A broken strategy on PEPE will
break on the next meme; a solid retest rule travels across liquid pairs. Compare spread to stop distance on
illiquid alts — if spread eats more than five percent of planned risk, choose a more liquid symbol or reduce size.

Open the lesson symbol at the stated interval. Mark swing highs and lows, sessions, or pattern boundaries with
horizontal lines and text labels for entry, stop, and target. Take one screenshot before any live order and store
it in your journal folder dated by session. Write setup name, planned R:R, and emotion score from one to ten before
entry. No screenshot means no trade — memory lies after three consecutive losses. Trade Master links lesson theory
to chart markup to journal review; signal groups skip the middle step and sell hope instead of process.

Limit orders add liquidity and often pay maker fee; market orders remove liquidity as taker — fees compound fast
for active scalpers. Funding every eight hours on perpetual contracts affects holds longer than one session.
Beginners use isolated margin only with modest leverage. On macro news days such as FOMC or CPI, stay flat thirty
minutes before release unless you have years of experience. Never average down without a written max loss in USDT
and an intact technical thesis. Daily loss limit and three-strike stop rule protect you from revenge spirals.

Trading without a stop, all-in on one illiquid coin, FOMO chase after missing a trigger, copying chat signals,
revenge size after a stop-out, moving stops wider to avoid being wrong, overtrading during fatigue hours after work.
Each mistake has a structural fix: checklist before click, cooldown timer after stop, tuition deposit size only,
weekly review by setup expectancy. If you cannot explain the trade idea to a friend in sixty seconds without
mentioning guaranteed profit, you are not ready to click live. Scam chats promise returns to harvest deposits;
legitimate education shows chart process and risk math instead. Protect API keys, enable 2FA, and never send USDT
to strangers for managed trading.

Close the lesson by opening TradingView on the lesson symbol, marking levels without clicking buy or sell,
and writing three sentences in your journal about what you would do if price reaches your trigger. Review
those notes in seven days — improvement in wording and patience often appears before balance growth.
Trading education is decision-making under uncertainty, not hunting lucky tickers. Every professional was
once a beginner with the same questions; the gap is journal discipline, defined stops, and refusing
third-party guarantees of profit. Schedule your next chart review on the calendar like a class — consistency
beats weekend binge studying. Share your written plan with an accountability partner if possible; explaining
aloud exposes gaps in logic before money is at risk.

Lesson 34 on FOMC Fed meeting trading rules is complete when you can explain the idea to a friend in two minutes
and show a marked TradingView screenshot with no open position. Log main takeaway, one risk you will remove
this week, and the date of your next plan review. Do not skip to the next lesson until practice steps are done.
In LATAM, fund via exchange P2P with escrow only; in Russia the same KYC path applies. Competitors may teach
savings habits; Trade Master teaches repeatable chart setups with honest statistics over time. Repeat the
weekly journal review even on weeks with zero trades — observation builds context. Keep position size small
until fifty logged trades follow your written rules. Paper trade or minimum risk when testing a new rule for the first week.
Market order for header text. Remove the stop “so that the front doesn’t get knocked out.” Double the size on the move.
Volatility remains for hours. Don't go back to full size the same day without a plan.
“The Fed is like a weather announcement for the entire market. During a storm I do not go to sea; I’ll watch where the wind blows when it calms down.”

Lesson 34 is complete when you can explain the topic “FOMC Meeting” to a friend in your own words in two minutes and show a screenshot of the markup in TradingView without an open position. Do not move on to the next lesson until you have completed the practice steps - otherwise the theory will be forgotten within a day.

sections.title: Step by step: creating a key on Binance

sections.body:
Step 1: Login to your Binance account, enable 2FA (Google Authenticator). You cannot create an API without 2FA.
Step 2. Profile → API Management → Create API. Sign the key: “Journal Read Only”.
Step 3. Go through verification. Copy the API Key and Secret. Secret is shown once -
save in your password manager, not in Telegram “Favorites”. Step 4. Edit restrictions: enable only
"Enable Reading". Uncheck Spot & Margin Trading, Futures, Withdrawals.
Step 5. If available - Restrict access to trusted IPs. Specify your home static IP or VPS IP.
Step 6. Do not send Secret to anyone. Don't commit to GitHub. If in doubt, delete the key and create a new one.
This checklist will take 10 minutes and will protect months of work on your deposit. If you still have questions after the block, write them down in one line in the journal and bring them to TradingView for analysis. Learning to trade is a marathon: today’s lesson builds on the previous ones (stop, R:R, structure, sessions).  In the Russian Federation, most people study through Binance P2P and USDT-M futures: the risk principles of 0.5–1% per transaction are the same for spot and futures. Don’t chase “secret indicators” from Telegram - a repeatable process is more important than another paid signal course.  In practice, open the lesson symbol at 15m and 4h: mark the last swing high and swing low - this is the basis for stops and targets. Without markings, any theory turns into fortune telling. A professional spends 20 minutes preparing and 2 minutes clicking – not the other way around.  Imagine explaining this to a friend over a cup of coffee: first in simple words, then with an example from a graph. It is important for a beginner not to learn the term by heart, but to see it on TradingView and write down the entry, stop and target before the click in the Trade Master journal. The goal of the course is not to “guess the coin,” but to trade according to a plan with limited risk.

Use read-only to audit risk per trade. Add trade API only after months of manual discipline. Open the lesson symbol on 15m and 4h; mark swing highs and lows — stops and targets start there.  In practice, mark the chart first, then decide entry, stop, and target before any click. Trade Master links each lesson to TradingView and a journal entry — not Telegram signal groups.  Risk 0.5–1% per trade applies on Binance spot and USDT-M futures alike. Process beats one lucky trade. Trade Master links each lesson to TradingView and a journal entry — not Telegram signal groups.  Risk 0.5–1% per trade applies on Binance spot and USDT-M futures alike. Process beats one lucky trade.

Scenario A: “Give me an API for free copy trading.” They often ask for Trade + sometimes Withdraw.
This is not copy trading, this is transferring control of the account to a stranger. Scenario B: Script with open
source on GitHub, but you haven't read the code. The Trojan can send Secret to the author's server.
Scenario B: One key for everything - journal, bot, TradingView, friend's test script. Compromise
one channel = compromise of everything. Scenario D: Screenshot from Secret in the support chat “help set up”.
Exchange support never asks for Secret. This is phishing. Remember the formula: 2FA + read-only +
without withdraw + separate keys + password manager + do not trust “guarantors” in Telegram. Compare with Finera and personal finance apps: they are about budgets and savings, and Trade Master is about a chart setup and an honest journal. These are different instruments; Do not confuse cost accounting with a trading plan for today.  A newbie mistake is to rush with the first deposit. It’s better to just watch the chart for three days and fill out a journal without making any trades than one evening of revenge after a minus. Discipline feels boring until you compare the score three months later.  If you still have questions after the block, write them down in one line in the journal and bring them to TradingView for analysis.
